summary_text: # Medical AI Adoption: Optimize for Traceable Judgment, Not Model Size **Summary** In medical AI, the highest leverage usually does not come from model size first. It comes from building shared context, auditability, and clear human responsibility boundaries so judgment stays legible, challengeable, and safe in high-stakes use. **Key Decisions** - Prioritize shared context and decision infrastructure before pursuing larger-model upgrades. Durable value comes from helping people and systems operate from the same evidence base. - Prefer systems that make judgment traceable. Recommendations should be tied to sources, rationale, and review history rather than presented as opaque outputs. - Keep human responsibility explicit at the decision boundary. AI can support interpretation and workflow acceleration, but it should not absorb accountability. - Avoid autonomy-first positioning in medical settings. Flashy claims are less valuable than infrastructure that preserves verification, oversight, and trust. **Open Questions** - What is the minimum audit trail required for each medical AI workflow? - Which decisions must always require human sign-off, and which can be safely delegated? - How should shared context be structured so clinicians, operators, and models work from the same ground truth? - What metrics should we use to evaluate adoption: automation rate, or trust, traceability, and clinical usefulness? consulted_worldview_source: /Users/masayuki/.claude/docs/philosophy.md consulted_paths: /Users/masayuki/.codex/skills/notion-knowledge-capture/SKILL.md,/Users/masayuki/.claude/CODEX.md,/Users/masayuki/.claude/docs/philosophy.md,/Users/masayuki/.codex/skills/notion-knowledge-capture/examples/decision-capture.md,/Users/masayuki/.codex/skills/notion-knowledge-capture/reference/documentation-database.md used_obsidian_for_worldview: no
